sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An acute dystonic reaction is an extrapyramidal side effect characterised by sudden involuntary abnormal postures or repetitive movements due to dopamine receptor blockade, often after antipsychotics or antiemetics.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Laryngospasm or upper-airway spasm with stridor or dyspnoea
- Oculogyric crisis or abnormal eye deviation
- Torticollis
- Opisthotonus
- Jaw spasm, tongue protrusion, dysarthria, grimacing
- Anxiety, agitation, diaphoresis, tachycardia
- Mental status is usually preserved

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- More common in younger patients and after dopamine-blocking medications such as metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, haloperidol, or risperidone
- Can be mistaken for seizure, meningitis, tetanus, anaphylaxis, or behavioural disturbance
- Medication history is essential

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position for airway support as clinically indicated
- Consider oxygen
- Be prepared to assist ventilations if required
- Consider ECG if diagnosis is unclear or significant medication exposure is suspected

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider vascular access
- Consider fluid resuscitation if indicated
- Consider IM/IV benztropine (1-2 mg) in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider promethazine (25-50 mg IM) in consultation with on-call doctor if appropriate
- Consider benzodiazepines for persistent or severe spasms in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Common acute dystonic patterns include oculogyric crisis, torticollis, jaw spasm, tongue protrusion, and opisthotonus
- Tardive dyskinesia and chorea are different movement disorders and should not be confused with acute dystonia
